key_points: Matter is composed of extremely small particles that cannot be seen directly
Particles of matter have spaces between them allowing mixing of substances
Particles of matter attract each other with varying strength
Particles are always in motion which causes diffusion
Different states of matter arise due to differences in particle arrangement and attraction
examples: Sugar dissolving in water shows that particles occupy spaces between water molecules
The smell of perfume spreading in air demonstrates diffusion of gas particles
Air compressed in a syringe demonstrates large spaces between gas particles
Potassium permanganate spreading in water shows particle movement in liquids
Melting of ice demonstrates weakening of interparticle attraction
formulas_or_processes: Diffusion: Movement of particles from higher concentration to lower concentration
Boiling: Liquid → Gas at boiling temperature
Evaporation: Liquid → Gas from surface at temperatures below boiling point
exam_tips: Understand how particle spacing explains compressibility of gases
Be able to explain diffusion using particle motion
Compare particle arrangement in solids, liquids, and gases
Revise experiments such as sugar dissolution and syringe compression
Use particle diagrams when explaining changes of state
